title: Classes and Objects
subtitle: Define classes and create objects in Python
learning_objectives: Define a class using the 'class' keyword
Set and read attributes directly on an object
Understand that each object is independent

sections.heading: Defining a Class
sections.content: Define a class with the class keyword, followed by the class name in PascalCase (each word starts with a capital), a colon, and an indented body. The simplest class body is pass.
sections.code_example:
class Dog:
    pass

# Create (instantiate) two Dog objects
rex = Dog()
bella = Dog()

print(type(rex))    # shows the class
print(rex == bella) # different objects, even though same class

sections.heading: Setting Attributes on Objects
sections.content: You can attach data to an object using dot notation: object.attribute = value. Each object stores its own copy of the data — changing one object does not affect another.
sections.code_example:
class Dog:
    pass

rex = Dog()
rex.name = "Rex"
rex.breed = "Labrador"
rex.age = 3

bella = Dog()
bella.name = "Bella"
bella.breed = "Poodle"
bella.age = 5

print(rex.name, "is a", rex.breed)
print(bella.name, "is", bella.age, "years old")
sections.code_output:
Rex is a Labrador
Bella is 5 years old

exercises.title: Create and Describe a Book
exercises.prompt: Define a class called Book (just pass). Create an object called book1, set its title to 'Python Basics', its author to 'Jane Smith', and its pages to 300. Print: 'Python Basics by Jane Smith, 300 pages'.
exercises.starter_code:
# Define the Book class


# Create book1 and set its attributes


# Print the details
exercises.solution:
class Book:
    pass

book1 = Book()
book1.title = "Python Basics"
book1.author = "Jane Smith"
book1.pages = 300

print(f"{book1.title} by {book1.author}, {book1.pages} pages")
hints: book1 = Book() to create the object
Use an f-string to print all three values

tests.value: Python Basics by Jane Smith, 300 pages
tests.message: Output: Python Basics by Jane Smith, 300 pages

exercises.title: Two Independent Objects
exercises.prompt: Create a class called Person. Make two Person objects: person1 with name='Alice' and age=30, person2 with name='Bob' and age=25. Print each person's name and age on separate lines.
exercises.starter_code:
class Person:
    pass

# Create person1 and person2


# Print both
exercises.solution:
class Person:
    pass

person1 = Person()
person1.name = "Alice"
person1.age = 30

person2 = Person()
person2.name = "Bob"
person2.age = 25

print(person1.name, person1.age)
print(person2.name, person2.age)
hints: Create two separate objects: person1 = Person()

quiz.question: What naming convention should class names use?
quiz.explanation: Python convention (PEP 8) says class names should use PascalCase — each word starts with a capital letter. For example: BankAccount, ShoppingCart, UserProfile.

quiz.question: If you create two objects from the same class and change an attribute on one, what happens to the other?
options: Nothing — each object has its own separate attributes
quiz.explanation: Each object is independent. Setting dog1.name = 'Rex' does not change dog2.name. Every object has its own copy of its attributes.

completion_message: Excellent! You can now define classes and create objects. Next up: the powerful __init__ method!
